description: Four agents manage a shared investment portfolio that must be rebalanced. Agent 1 (Risk): updates risk exposure limits and VaR calculations. Agent 2 (Allocation): updates asset allocation percentages across equities, bonds, and cash. Agent 3 (Compliance): updates regulatory constraint flags and position limits. Agent 4 (Reporting): updates the executive summary narrative. All four agents share a single portfolio_state shard. Conflicts arise when Risk changes limits that invalidate Allocation's percentages, or when Compliance constrains positions that Allocation already committed to.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 sets max_equity=60%. Agent 2 (reading stale v0) allocates equity=75%. These are mutually exclusive.
ground_truth_checks: Equity allocation is within stated risk limits
Sum of all allocations equals 100%
No position exceeds compliance limits
Narrative is consistent with allocations

description: Four agents collaboratively update a shared patient medical record. Agent 1 (Diagnosis): records primary and differential diagnoses. Agent 2 (Treatment): writes treatment plan and medication orders. Agent 3 (Nursing): documents nursing interventions and vital sign thresholds. Agent 4 (Discharge): prepares discharge planning notes. All agents share a single patient_record shard. Conflicts arise when Treatment prescribes a medication that Nursing's allergy list forbids, or when Discharge plans a date that Treatment's timeline contradicts.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 diagnoses severe hypertension. Agent 2 (stale) prescribes NSAIDs which raise blood pressure. These are medically contradictory.
ground_truth_checks: Medications are consistent with diagnoses
No prescribed medication appears in allergy/contraindication list
Discharge timeline is consistent with treatment duration
Nursing thresholds match treatment targets

description: Four agents design a shared REST API schema that must be internally consistent. Agent 1 (API): defines endpoint paths, HTTP methods, and response codes. Agent 2 (Database): defines the data model, table schemas, and foreign keys. Agent 3 (Auth): defines authentication requirements per endpoint and JWT claims. Agent 4 (Docs): writes the OpenAPI specification narrative. All agents share a single api_schema shard. Conflicts arise when API adds an endpoint that Database has no table for, or when Auth requires a claim that API does not return.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 adds GET /users/{id}/orders endpoint. Agent 2 (stale) deletes the orders table. These are structurally inconsistent.
ground_truth_checks: Every API endpoint has a corresponding database table
Auth requirements reference only fields that endpoints return
OpenAPI docs match defined endpoints
No undefined foreign key references

description: Four agents collaborate to produce a Kubernetes deployment configuration that must be consistent across components. Agent 1 (App): sets container image versions, resource requests/limits, and environment variables. Agent 2 (Network): sets service ports, ingress rules, and load balancer configuration. Agent 3 (Storage): sets PersistentVolumeClaim specs and mount paths. Agent 4 (Security): sets RBAC roles, pod security contexts, and secret references. All agents share a single k8s_config shard. Conflicts arise when App defines an env var referencing a Secret that Security has not created, or when Network opens a port that App does not listen on.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 sets DATABASE_URL=secret/db-creds. Agent 3 (stale) does not mount the secret at that path. This would cause pod startup failure.
ground_truth_checks: All referenced secrets exist in the security configuration
All mounted volumes are defined in PVC specs
Network ports match container port declarations
RBAC roles grant only what is needed

description: Four agents collaboratively draft a shared research paper outline that must be internally consistent. Agent 1 (Introduction): writes the problem statement, motivation, and contribution list. Agent 2 (Methods): writes the methodology section including experimental design and baselines. Agent 3 (Results): writes the results section referencing specific experiments and tables. Agent 4 (Discussion): writes the discussion and limitations sections. All agents share a single paper_outline shard. Conflicts arise when Results references an experiment that Methods has not defined, or when Discussion claims a result that Results does not show.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 lists Contribution C3 as 'formal proof for arbitrary N'. Agent 4 (stale) claims C3 is 'empirical scaling study'. These are contradictory contribution statements.
ground_truth_checks: Contributions listed in Introduction match those validated in Results
Every experiment referenced in Results is defined in Methods
Limitations in Discussion match the scope stated in Introduction
No circular claims between sections

description: Four agents produce a shared quarterly financial report that must be numerically consistent. Agent 1 (Revenue): records revenue by product line and region. Agent 2 (Expenses): records operating expenses, COGS, and depreciation. Agent 3 (Projections): writes forward guidance and analyst targets. Agent 4 (Narrative): writes the CEO letter and MD&A section. All agents share a single financial_report shard. Conflicts arise when Revenue records Q3 revenue as $12.4M but Narrative says $14.2M, or when Expenses' EBITDA differs from Projections' baseline.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 sets Q3_revenue=12.4M. Agent 4 (stale) writes 'revenue grew to $14.2M'. These are numerically inconsistent.
ground_truth_checks: All revenue figures are consistent across sections
EBITDA = Revenue - Expenses (within rounding)
Projections baseline matches reported actuals
Narrative percentages match computed deltas

description: Four agents collaborate on a shared incident response runbook for a production outage. Agent 1 (Detection): documents monitoring alerts, thresholds, and escalation triggers. Agent 2 (Mitigation): documents immediate mitigation steps including rollback procedures. Agent 3 (RCA): documents root cause analysis steps and diagnostic commands. Agent 4 (Communication): writes stakeholder communication templates and status page updates. All agents share a single runbook shard. Conflicts arise when Mitigation's rollback target version differs from Detection's known-good version, or when Communication describes a resolution step that Mitigation has not defined.
conflict_scenario: Agent 2 specifies rollback to v2.3.1 as mitigation. Agent 4 (stale) writes 'rolling back to v2.2.8' in the communication template. These are contradictory.
ground_truth_checks: Rollback version is consistent across all sections
Communication templates reference only defined mitigation steps
RCA diagnostic commands are consistent with Detection alerts
Escalation thresholds match monitoring configuration

description: Four agents define a shared microservice contract between an order service and an inventory service. Agent 1 (Order Service): defines the events the order service publishes and the APIs it exposes. Agent 2 (Inventory Service): defines the events the inventory service consumes and the stock reservation API. Agent 3 (Schema): defines the shared Protobuf/JSON schemas for all events and API payloads. Agent 4 (SLA): defines latency SLOs, retry policies, and circuit breaker thresholds. All agents share a single service_contract shard. Conflicts arise when Order publishes an event that Schema has not defined, or when Inventory's retry policy exceeds Order's SLO.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 publishes order.created with field customer_email. Agent 3 (stale) defines order.created schema without customer_email field. Contract is broken.
ground_truth_checks: All published events have matching schema definitions
All consumed events are published by the counterpart service
Retry budgets do not exceed SLO thresholds
API payload fields match schema definitions

description: Four agents collaboratively configure a shared ML training pipeline that must be internally consistent. Agent 1 (Data): specifies data preprocessing steps, feature engineering, and train/val/test splits. Agent 2 (Model): specifies model architecture, hyperparameters, and optimizer settings. Agent 3 (Training): specifies training loop, learning rate schedule, and early stopping criteria. Agent 4 (Evaluation): specifies evaluation metrics, thresholds for deployment, and A/B test design. All agents share a single pipeline_config shard. Conflicts arise when Data's feature dimension differs from Model's input layer, or when Training's epoch budget contradicts Evaluation's convergence criteria.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 produces 128-dimensional feature vectors. Agent 2 (stale) defines input layer with 64 dimensions. Shape mismatch causes training failure.
ground_truth_checks: Feature dimensions match model input layer dimensions
Train/val/test splits sum to 100%
Learning rate schedule is compatible with optimizer
Evaluation metrics are appropriate for the task type

description: Four agents collaboratively draft a shared SaaS service agreement that must be legally consistent. Agent 1 (Terms): writes the service description, SLA commitments, and uptime guarantees. Agent 2 (Liability): writes the limitation of liability clauses and indemnification terms. Agent 3 (Data): writes the data processing agreement, retention policies, and GDPR compliance clauses. Agent 4 (Termination): writes the termination conditions, notice periods, and data deletion procedures. All agents share a single contract_draft shard. Conflicts arise when Terms commits to 99.9% uptime but Liability caps claims at $0 for downtime, or when Termination's data deletion timeline contradicts Data's retention policy.
conflict_scenario: Agent 1 guarantees 99.99% uptime with service credits. Agent 2 (stale) caps all liability at $100 with no service credit provision. These are contradictory obligations.
ground_truth_checks: Liability caps are consistent with SLA credit commitments
Data retention periods do not exceed GDPR maximum
Termination notice periods are consistent across sections
Uptime guarantees have corresponding remedy provisions
